skill.name: wireshark-analysis
skill.description: Guides authorized Wireshark packet capture and PCAP analysis for troubleshooting, protocol review, and defensive security investigations.
skill.summary: Analyze authorized network captures with Wireshark filters, stream reconstruction, statistics, and security-focused review steps.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static command-execution findings are false positives caused by fenced Markdown examples and Wireshark filter syntax, not executable Ruby or shell code. The skill has legitimate defensive intent, but it requires a medium risk warning because it guides live packet capture, credential discovery in traffic, HTTP object export, and TLS key material handling.

medium_findings.title: Sensitive Packet Capture and Credential Review Workflow
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs users to capture network traffic, follow streams, search for credentials, and investigate plaintext login submissions. This is legitimate for authorized incident response, but captured PCAPs can contain passwords, session tokens, personal data, and confidential business traffic.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The guide directly references packet capture privileges, credential searches, and handling captured sensitive data. The surrounding guardrails indicate defensive use, so the issue is data exposure risk rather than confirmed malicious intent.
medium_findings.remediation: Publish with a warning requiring authorization, minimization, secure PCAP storage, and redaction before sharing outputs.

medium_findings.title: TLS Private Key and Pre-Master Secret Handling
medium_findings.description: The troubleshooting section tells users to obtain a server private key or capture browser pre-master secrets for TLS decryption. These materials are highly sensitive and could expose encrypted sessions if mishandled.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The sensitive key-material guidance is explicit and line-specific. It is framed as TLS troubleshooting, so it is not malicious by itself, but it creates clear operational risk.
medium_findings.remediation: Require users to handle TLS keys only with written authorization and to avoid uploading key material or decrypted captures to untrusted systems.

medium_findings.title: Live Network Monitoring Requires Authorization
medium_findings.description: The skill covers live capture setup, capture filters, and analysis of traffic outside trusted networks. Unauthorized packet capture can violate privacy, policy, or law even when the technical steps are normal Wireshark use.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The skill explicitly guides live capture and broad traffic filtering, and it also states operational boundaries. This supports a medium operational risk rather than a publication block.
medium_findings.remediation: Publish with clear authorization and privacy warnings for live captures and shared PCAP artifacts.

low_findings.title: Static Command Execution Findings Are Markdown False Positives
low_findings.description: The analyzer flagged many fenced code blocks as Ruby or shell backtick execution. Review found Wireshark GUI steps, display filters, capture filters, and menu paths inside Markdown, with no executable script file or dynamic command invocation.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced lines are Markdown fences and Wireshark syntax examples, not code executed by the skill. No evidence found of a script, shell invocation, or command injection path.
low_findings.remediation: No code remediation is required; marketplace warnings should focus on packet-capture data handling instead.

low_findings.title: Defensive Security Keywords Are Contextual False Positives
low_findings.description: C2, malware, SSL, and protocol keywords appear in defensive Wireshark analysis examples. The content describes detection and troubleshooting rather than payload deployment, exploitation, or command-and-control operation.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The suspicious terms are embedded in protocol filter references and a malware traffic detection scenario. No evidence found of instructions to build, deploy, or control malware.
low_findings.remediation: Keep the defensive context and authorization guidance visible in marketplace metadata.

content.value_statement: Network captures can be hard to interpret during incidents or outages. This skill provides structured Wireshark workflows for authorized packet capture, filtering, stream review, and evidence documentation.
actual_capabilities: Explains how to start authorized live captures and choose capture interfaces.
Provides practical capture and display filters for hosts, ports, protocols, flags, DNS, and HTTP.
Guides TCP, UDP, HTTP, and TLS stream reconstruction in Wireshark.
Uses Wireshark statistics, conversations, endpoints, flow graphs, and I/O graphs for traffic review.
Supports defensive checks for port scans, ARP spoofing, suspicious DNS, downloads, and beacon-like traffic.
Includes troubleshooting steps for empty captures, invalid filters, performance issues, and TLS decryption limits.
limitations: Requires Wireshark, packet capture permissions, and user knowledge of the target network.
Does not automate packet parsing or run analysis scripts by itself.
Cannot decrypt modern TLS traffic without authorized keys or session secrets.
May expose sensitive data in PCAP files if users do not minimize and protect captures.

use_cases.title: Investigate Suspicious Traffic
use_cases.description: Review DNS, HTTP, TCP flags, streams, and timing patterns to support authorized incident response.

use_cases.title: Diagnose Network Performance Issues
use_cases.description: Use retransmission filters, I/O graphs, conversations, and expert information to find latency or packet loss evidence.

use_cases.title: Teach Packet Analysis Basics
use_cases.description: Walk learners through common filters, protocols, streams, and Wireshark views using structured examples.

prompt_templates.title: Start a Basic Capture
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me capture authorized traffic in Wireshark for a connection issue. Include interface selection, safe capture filters, and what to record.

prompt_templates.title: Filter a PCAP by Host and Protocol
prompt_templates.scenario: Focused review of an existing capture.
prompt_templates.prompt: I have a PCAP from an authorized investigation. Show me Wireshark display filters for one host, DNS, HTTP, TCP errors, and suspicious ports.

prompt_templates.title: Analyze a Suspicious Conversation
prompt_templates.prompt: Guide me through following TCP and UDP streams for a suspicious host. Include what indicators to document and how to avoid exposing secrets.

prompt_templates.title: Build an Incident Analysis Workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a Wireshark workflow for malware traffic triage in an authorized PCAP. Include DNS patterns, beacon timing, exports, statistics, and reporting steps.

output_examples.input: Analyze slow access to a web application from a PCAP.
output: A filter plan for the web server, HTTP or TLS traffic, retransmissions, resets, and response timing.
A review checklist using Conversations, Expert Information, and I/O Graphs.
A short findings format for packet loss, latency, and server response evidence.
A cautious HTTP POST and stream review workflow for authorized captures.
Guidance to redact usernames, passwords, tokens, and session identifiers before sharing results.
A report outline that separates evidence, scope, and remediation recommendations.

output_examples.input: Look for suspicious DNS and beaconing behavior.
output: DNS query filters and statistics views to identify unusual domains or repeated timing.
Conversation and endpoint checks for destination concentration and unexpected ports.
A defensible summary of indicators without claiming malware without corroborating evidence.

best_practices: Capture only traffic you are authorized to inspect, and minimize scope with capture filters.
Store PCAP files securely because they can include credentials, tokens, and private communications.
Document filters, timestamps, endpoints, and assumptions so findings can be reproduced.
anti_patterns: Do not capture third-party traffic without permission or a defined operational need.
Do not upload raw PCAPs, TLS keys, passwords, or session secrets to untrusted services.
Do not treat one suspicious filter match as proof of compromise without corroborating evidence.

faq.question: Does this skill run Wireshark automatically?
faq.answer: No. It is a procedural guide for using Wireshark views, filters, and analysis workflows.

faq.question: Can it analyze encrypted TLS content?
faq.answer: Only when authorized TLS keys or session secrets are available. Many modern sessions cannot be decrypted passively.

faq.question: Is live packet capture safe?
faq.answer: It is safe only within authorized scope. Packet captures can include private data and must be protected.

faq.question: What files does it support?
faq.answer: The guidance focuses on PCAP and PCAPNG files opened in Wireshark.

faq.question: Can it help with malware traffic?
faq.answer: Yes, for defensive review of authorized captures. It focuses on indicators such as DNS anomalies and beacon timing.

faq.question: What should I include in a final report?
faq.answer: Include scope, capture time, filters used, endpoints, observed behavior, evidence notes, and privacy redactions.
